label: premortem-position
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: state, in a few sentences, the position or course of action the
agency's counsel would MOST LIKELY adopt here. Not the best position — the
likely one. No analysis beyond what is needed to state it.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Vista Camino (invented; demographics roughly 78% non-Hispanic white against a metro region at 52%) owns a 4.2-acre former corporation yard appraised at $11.8M. The council approved, 4–1, an exclusive negotiating agreement and then a disposition and development agreement conveying the parcel to Pacifica Communities LLC, a private developer, for $1, in exchange for: 120 housing units of which 30% (36 units) are deed-restricted affordable — 18 at low-income and 18 at moderate-income levels — with 25-year covenants; a half-acre publicly accessible plaza built and maintained by the developer; and a "community preference" covenant giving 50% of the affordable units priority to applicants who have lived or worked in Vista Camino for at least five years.

The city declared the parcel "exempt surplus land" and did not send notices of availability to housing sponsors before entering exclusive negotiations. The Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) has issued a notice of violation asserting the disposition does not qualify for the claimed exemption (citing the affordability mix and the 25-year covenant term) and warning of penalties of 30% of the disposition price or fair market value. A taxpayer association has sued alleging an unconstitutional gift of public funds (Cal. Const. art. XVI, § 6) given the $11.8M-to-$1 spread, and a regional fair-housing organization has threatened suit alleging the five-year local preference has an unlawful disparate impact given the city's demographics.

## The question

Does the disposition violate the Surplus Land Act (Gov. Code §§ 54220–54234) — was the parcel eligible for any 'exempt surplus land' category given the 30% low/moderate mix and 25-year covenants, what process was owed, and what do HCD's enforcement position and the 30% penalty mean for the deal? Is $1 plus in-kind public benefits adequate consideration under the gift clause, or is the public-purpose doctrine enough? Does the local-preference covenant violate the FHA/FEHA under a disparate-impact theory or AFFH obligations, and can it be saved by redesign? How do the three exposures interact, and how should the deal be restructured?

What position would the agency's counsel most likely adopt?

likely_position.description: the position/course of action a cautious public-agency counsel would most likely recommend, in 2-4 sentences

text: {"likely_position":"Counsel would most likely treat HCD's notice of violation as the highest-probability loss — the 25-year covenant term (short of the ~45-55 year durations required under Gov. Code § 54221(f)/(g) exemption categories) and the affordability mix make the \"exempt surplus land\" claim weak — and would recommend curing rather than litigating: pause performance, negotiate a compliance/workout agreement with HCD (extending covenants, revisiting the mix, and/or restarting notice-of-availability procedures under §§ 54222–54223 with housing sponsors) to avoid the 30% penalty exposure. On the gift-clause claim, counsel would likely defend rather than settle, citing the public-purpose doctrine and in-kind consideration (affordable units, public plaza) as adequate under cases like *County of Alameda v. Janssen* and the deference courts give to legislative findings of public benefit, viewing this as the lowest-risk exposure. On the local-preference covenant, counsel would likely get ahead of the threatened FHA/FEHA suit proactively — narrowing the five-year preference (lower than 50%, or reframe as displacement/anti-displacement based rather than broad residency-based) before litigation is filed, since disparate-impact exposure given the city's demographics (per *Texas Dep't of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project*) is seen as real but curable by redesign. Overall, the likely institutional posture is: cure the SLA problem administratively with HCD, defend the gift-clause claim in litigation if sued, and quietly amend the preference covenant to reduce fair-housing risk — all while advising the council that the deal is salvageable through DDA amendment rather than rescission."}

data.likely_position: Counsel would most likely treat HCD's notice of violation as the highest-probability loss — the 25-year covenant term (short of the ~45-55 year durations required under Gov. Code § 54221(f)/(g) exemption categories) and the affordability mix make the "exempt surplus land" claim weak — and would recommend curing rather than litigating: pause performance, negotiate a compliance/workout agreement with HCD (extending covenants, revisiting the mix, and/or restarting notice-of-availability procedures under §§ 54222–54223 with housing sponsors) to avoid the 30% penalty exposure. On the gift-clause claim, counsel would likely defend rather than settle, citing the public-purpose doctrine and in-kind consideration (affordable units, public plaza) as adequate under cases like *County of Alameda v. Janssen* and the deference courts give to legislative findings of public benefit, viewing this as the lowest-risk exposure. On the local-preference covenant, counsel would likely get ahead of the threatened FHA/FEHA suit proactively — narrowing the five-year preference (lower than 50%, or reframe as displacement/anti-displacement based rather than broad residency-based) before litigation is filed, since disparate-impact exposure given the city's demographics (per *Texas Dep't of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project*) is seen as real but curable by redesign. Overall, the likely institutional posture is: cure the SLA problem administratively with HCD, defend the gift-clause claim in litigation if sued, and quietly amend the preference covenant to reduce fair-housing risk — all while advising the council that the deal is salvageable through DDA amendment rather than rescission.
